sentences.text: The death march?
No, it could be in a half an hour.
How--
Yes.
I would like very much because I-- just, you know, we're talking and so many things happened in one day, in one hour, in one minute.
So it's hard to-- to-- you know, to get to everything.
But I would actually like to do.

We had in Auschwitz, in Birkenau, when I was still in the-- in the camp with the rest of the population-- I would say the inmates population, the inmates together-- besides us Jews-- I mentioned there were Aryan too.
There were Polish women.
There were German brought in extra who were for political reasons there.
And there were also Jehovah's Witnesses, you know, [INAUDIBLE], but they were very privileged.
They didn't-- they worked as maids or nannies in the households of the SS.
And they were all too women from quite a large group, women from the Ukraine.
The Ukraine women, they were exposed already to very, very harsh life during the Communist era in Russia.
So they were really survivors.
But they worked in separate units, not with Jews together.
But the Germans treated them even worse like Jews.
That's-- that's-- I have to say.
They were getting the worst jobs, carrying demolition houses, carrying stones, working the hardest job in the factories.
But beside being in the same place with Jews, with us, they were very, very, very much anti-Semitic, even in Birkenau.
If some of the Ukrainians-- you know, it happened that they worked together with Jewish laborers.
They were always mean, and if they could hit or torture a Jewish girl, they did.

I had to mention them because I remember that one of my girlfriends worked also-- close to Auschwitz, there was a-- a little village, and they had to tore down the houses because they-- I don't know if they built something else or not.
So she told me that these Ukrainian women-- and they were about, she said, 10 in this kommando-- they were worse than the German kapos there.
And she suffered this time physically very much from them.
It's only-- the one time what I heard about this-- what I saw after zahlappell when-- that-- [? they ?] [? were-- ?] because they were dressed even different.

They had the striped uniforms, and we and the people who came already-- like I said, the Hungarian to camp-- they had civilian clothing with the cross-- a red, you know, cross in the back so you could recognize them even, you know, they were Caucasian, white like the rest.
It's not like here-- the races in color.
So just that, what I want to say about the Ukrainians.
Although, you know, how-- how very sorry we were toward those poor Russian POWs.
They were mixed-- Ukraine, [?
Belarus, ?] Latvians, in one together who were killed, you know, by the thousands before we came.
So you're saying the some of the Ukrainian women had the worst jobs--
Yes.
They did.
But other Ukrainian women were in the status of, like, a kapo, also.
No.
They were not kapo.
They were foremens, but not kapos.
Kapo-- a kapo was a head of the foremen, and we were, you know, the workers, the slave workers.
But they worked too-- no compare.
And they suffered the same, you know, destiny like we do it-- we did.
But they did not gas the Aryans.
Only the Jews were gassed.
And later on, when, you know-- after '44, after the uprising, the gassing stopped.
So many of them were killed in the Black-- at the Black Wall in Auschwitz between barracks 10 and 11 or in the woods up to Brzezinka.
Well, it happened after, you know, the Hungarian came.
It happened in September.
The Hungarian transports came from May until September.
What year? 1944.
And, like I said, thousands and thousands.
But I will go back to the SS guard because I didn't finish what he did in the camp, why, you know, I was so anxious to testify [? again ?] him.
He was-- well, like I mentioned, Gottfried [?
Weisser ?] was his name.
He returned after the war.
He was an SS in Auschwitz.
But he came to Auschwitz-- he was on the front, and he lost an eye and other injuries.
After, you know, recuperating, he got a job in Auschwitz. and he had one glass eye.

His specialty in Auschwitz was-- in Birkenau-- in Birkenau, not in Auschwitz-- in men's camp, when he saw a young male prisoner working not far away, he stopped him, had to find an empty can of food-- you know, from food-- because they were there-- had to put the boy in the middle of the lagerstrasse, an empty can on his head, and he was shooting-- only young, very young boys.
And when he didn't-- when he didn't shoot somebody in his body, again the can.
And then he shoot him in his forehead.
The man fell and bleeding.
Had to get up and again put up the can.
And he did it as long he couldn't move anymore and couldn't lift the can.
And then he stepped on his body and still was shooting.
He was called, in the camp, and well known-- they gave him a name Wilhelm Tell.
You know the apple and this.
So we well known as Wilhelm Tell.
Besides this, they called him also slepoy.
You know what slepoy means?
Blind.
It's a Slavic expression even in Russian.
And when in [? 1960 ?] I was called here, in San Francisco, to the German consulate, they want-- they had someone already, you know, detained.
And they were looking for eyewitnesses.
And like, you know, we-- from the survivors-- many are registered-- not we, the Red Cross in Germany-- has a registry because when we came, we all were registered.
Not later, but the first transport.
And they know who was there.
And through the Jewish communities-- [?
Wiesenthal ?] Center-- when I came here, even before, I was registered as a survivor.
And at the Jewish agencies, every-- just to look at it-- who was in our-- who survived Auschwitz.
So I was there at the German consulate.
They gave me a huge album-- if I could recognize him.
And they didn't tell me the name.
He said somebody's [? in ?] detained.
Didn't say where and what-- if I could recognize a certain man who was in Auschwitz.
I said, well, it's almost 50 years.
I try.
And I went through.
I said, I'm very sorry.
I can't do it.
And-- but, you know, the consulate insisted.
He said, go through every picture.
And take your time.
So I did.
Then I pointed at one.
He was standing straight up in his SS uniform.
And I said, he's very, very familiar with me, but I can't recall where I met him.
And then, again, in civilian clothing, photograph from the side, from the front, different, you know from different angles.
And I recognized him again from the side, like from the profile.
And again, he said, you know, are you sure you recognize?
I said, yes.
I [? may-- ?] I think so.
Very, very familiar.
And then, they told me who he was.
He said he was an SS in Auschwitz.
Oh, I think so.
I know the face.
And then he told me his name.
I said, no.
But something-- [? what ?] I know is wrong with his eyes.
I still didn't know.
I said, the eyes, something wrong.
And then he told me, he's Gottfried [?
Weisser. ?] He was in Auschwitz.
His name is so and so.
I said, but I don't know the name because he didn't introduce himself to us.
I know someone who was called slepoy.
It was him.
And so in 1987, one year later, I was called to Wuppertal to testify.
And I went.
I do.
I will.
In 1944 when, you know, the Hungarian transports started coming and coming.
And it was thousands of [? it. ?] Very few went to live-- were selected as slave laborers better to say.
And the rest all were slaughtered there.
I mentioned already what happened, what I saw with my eyes.
And then in-- [? now, ?] it was in October-- start, beginning of October-- I had, I think so, the night shift.
And early in the morning, we had to, you know, change because when we marched to our barracks-- because it was in the same place-- the other shift marched already to take our place for the day shift.
And suddenly, a big blast.
The crematorium was blown up.
And the sirens started, the SS running around.
In no time, hundreds of SS men-- they brought in on-- on trucks.
And the sonderkommando, they were 600 boys, young boys.
The sonderkommando, they always changed.
When new arrivals come, they gassed all the sonderkommando many times.
And younger people, stronger, they-- they pushed into this kommando.
And the sonderkommando started running.

Like I've mentioned, between our working place and the crematorium where the sonderkommando lived on top-- they lived in the same place where they worked-- tore through the-- broke through the barbed wire.
But it was not voltage.
And many of them were hiding in those piles of clothing.
And we had to stay outside, all the Kanada unit, the working unit, worked there.
And shivering and, you know, the screaming and yelling of the-- of the dogs.
And they went searching every barrack.
While they searched the barracks, naturally, they found in hiding two boys from the sonderkommando.

Then the SS-- and it was Wunsch and Graf, which I mentioned, which I testified against them-- were dragging the two men by their hand and beating them out of the-- of the gate from where we were working and beating them and kicking them.
And they killed them.
And searching for the rest of the sonderkommando.
In the meantime, you know, it was fire.
And it was really very, very scary.
We knew now is the end of us.
Absolutely the end.
But what happened, you know, late in the day, they again found all the sonderkommando which was working there.
They shot them all.
They killed them.
And from this time, it was no more gassing.
The [? blast-- ?] the sonderkommando.
But, you know, what I learned later how they got [NON-ENGLISH] the-- the-- the-- how you say it?
The dynamite.
You know the girls from the union factory?
You know, a long time, they were smuggling in little, tiny things-- the-- the gun [? power ?] to the sonderkommando.
The sonderkommando had no kitchen for itself.
They had to bring in the food from the kitchen.
But when they bring in the food, they got in contact with other people because the sonderkommando had to pick up the food.
Besides this, like I mentioned, Erik Kolka, he was a plumber.
And many times, he had to repair something there too.
So I don't know if he smuggled too or not.
But the four girls on January-- I don't know which-- beginning of January, before the death march-- they caught them.
They interrogated the girls who worked in the powder room.
You know, Laura Shelly has a book about that, That was her-- that is her last book.
She-- there I read the whole story how it happened, how the gun powder was smuggled in to the sonderkommando, and how it was.
The four girls were hung.
They hung them.
And the whole story how the-- they worked-- how you say the term of-- of it, you know, behind-- in-- in Birkenau-- the Resistance.
They were the Resistance [? where ?] people whom nobody knew.
And me, personal, I never knew even about the Resistance.
First of all, I was so separated from the rest of the inmates.
And I somehow was not involved in anything like that.
The only thing what we smuggled little papers to the Germans-- 'Watch out, Achtung.' You know, Jewish clothing.
Yes?
So it's from this time on, it was not gassing, but it doesn't mean transport didn't came.
The transport stopped too, but here and there, they brought some people, you know, 30, 40 people, which they killed or-- or-- or you know, they let some of them in.
But the-- the-- the mass killing was over.
Who were the last people to be--
Hungarian transport.
The Hungarian transport.
Personally, I can't point at any of them.
The last mass [INAUDIBLE] killings was in October 1944.
But the killing went on, and the burning we still saw.
But not such-- in such masses like they did before.
But we-- you know, then-- I was already the third-- it was already the third winter what I spent in Auschwitz.
It was also a very, very cold winter.
Here and there, you know, from transports, when we sorted the clothing, we found pieces of newspaper.
So we saw the war is coming to an end.
Besides this, you know, Auschwitz was bombarded too.

But we who were already better fed and could [? think-- ?] I thought [? of ?] how is it possible that those-- you know, we saw lots of-- of air, you know, planes going over our heads towards Russia-- toward Germany already, not Russia.
Towards Germany.
The Russians were already very advanced.
And they didn't bomb.
But they bombed quite a few times, but never the camp.
And we were praying for it.
At least some of us have a chance to escape, to go.
But from the escapees, I have to mention it too.
I forgot.
It was only once very, very successful-- three escapees from Slovakia.
And they were good friends of ours.
We know them very well.
In 1944-- I don't remember the month-- three people escaped the same time.
It was Fredo [?
Wechsler ?] from Slovakia, [?
Peter ?] [?
Mondovich, ?] [?
Rosen, ?] and one from [?
Turnov-- ?] four actually-- I don't know exactly the name.
He's a professor of science in Toronto University.
I don't know if he's retired, but he be here as a guest speaker at Yom Hashoah.
You will see him.
We know him.
It's same transport with Fred-- [?
Fredo, ?] [?
Peter, ?] and [?
Rosen. ?]
We didn't.
How could we know if those are Russians?
But [? with ?] [? the ?] Allied, we didn't know.
But you know, the union worker was hit and two girls dormitories were [INAUDIBLE].
I think so, one girl was killed, or two.
But Birkenau, not once.
In Auschwitz, like the camp, the men's camp, was not hit, not once.
Around-- but they didn't hit the union or [INAUDIBLE] workers because the monopoly-- the Americans had your stocks in it.
So I didn't know then.
And ?] I don't know why they didn't bomb.
They want-- didn't want to destroy the Americans either.
The-- they had properties there.
And this time, [?
Fredo ?] [?
Wechsler-- ?] but [?
Fredo ?] [?
Wechsler, ?] when they came out, they sent a letter through [INAUDIBLE] in Slovakia to the pope.
And, you know, one letter when to America-- what's going on there, and they are escapees-- and one to London.
But they didn't publish it for a long, long time.
And they didn't believe it.
To the government, to the London government and here to America to the president.
They didn't believe it, or they didn't want to believe it.
They probably knew.
And also, they warned Hungary what's going on in here to see that the people can escape and not in the transports should revolt because they are going to [INAUDIBLE].
They didn't believe it either.
They didn't believe it.
So I just mentioned those true brave-- those really true, brave guys who came back alive from hell.
We, too.
I mean, me, too.
But they escaped from the camp, from Birkenau.
So what indication did you have that the war was ending?

Because-- first of all, very soon, you know, in-- in 1944 already, in-- before January, before we went to through the death march, we saw already here and there [INAUDIBLE], you know, fire because the Russians were in-- [INAUDIBLE]-- in Krakow.
And Krakow is only 60 kilometers from Auschwitz.
And-- and, you know, I forgot to mention-- you see I'm already almost done with the camp-- the transports, and Mengele, and the twins.
I was not part of them, but I know what was going on.

The, you know, the-- and then, the [? block ?] [? 10 ?] where Renee was, the block where they had Guinea pigs made from-- from the Jewish inmates, where they, you know, all kinds of-- of-- how you say-- experiments on them.
The experiment block, Block 10, where I was first housed in Auschwitz was with Dr.
Clauberg, head of it, who was sentenced, you know, in Nuremberg, and other famous doctors who were assistants of him tortured thousands of Jewish women in different experiments.
And then, you had, you know, all kinds of different experiments done even elsewhere in the camp which I don't know about it.
And Dr.
Mengele, the famous Angel of Death, you know, what-- what he did the experiments on twins.
And some of those twins survived.
I just heard them on the television talking.
And well, all kinds of inhuman things what went on there, which, you know, I don't even recall at [? the ?] [? spur of the ?] moment because I have so much what I saw.
And I can hardly come to-- even I will talk to you, you know, days and days.
You know, one Czech, very famous poet what I mentioned, which I [? gave ?] the book.
It was published in 1946 in the publishing company where I was an employee.
And the motto, you know, the first page of the-- it was picture book of children who were also killed.
And the title of the book was, You see the whole book.

It's-- it's a beautiful-- I mean, a beautiful-- well done photographs, which I don't know where they found them-- children from all over Europe-- from Yugoslavia, from the Ukraine-- because they didn't [? got ?] to Russia.
They were only in the Ukraine.
From everywhere.
From the ghettos.
Czechoslovakia, a publishing company, [NON-ENGLISH], where I was working for 15 years.
So it was only published, I think so, 3,000.
And I-- when we came here, I brought the book.
And later when the Holocaust library was formed, I gave them about 10, 15 books, all from camp because the company where I was working published all those books.
And I got them right away.
So-- and there was a letter from a little Polish boy from a ghetto who said-- just what I recall.
He writes a letter to his parents because he was caught somewhere else.
I am here in this miserable place.
I have no shoes.
I'm hungry.
My clothes-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my clothes are torn.
I am hungry.
The only thing I want to be with you, but I can't write so much.
If the oceans were ink and the skies paper, I couldn't describe the horror of what I'm going through.
But it's quite a long letter.
In our company, those picture book of the killed children.
So I just want to say even children were killed there, which I mentioned already, which I saw.
And the big blessing for me-- the big blessing for me is that I'm here alive and able to talk about it and remembering.
And I hope so that never ever something like that will happen again.
And so we are through now, almost.
And so, like I say, it was the third winter-- cold and freezing.
We already had some knowledge that the end of the war is soon coming.

But we probably won't see it-- freedom-- ever because the SS there in our kommando, where we had better condition than the rest, promised that the only way to freedom for us is through the chimney and pointed at it.
So we were sure we never will survive because [? we ?] special-- knew too much and saw too much.
